searchResults.name: Bridal Veil Falls and Lunch Rock via Lake Serene Trail
searchResults.location_label: Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest, Washington
searchResults.description:
Lake Serene is an alpine lake nestled in the Cascade mountains of Washington State. Lake Serene Trail is a popular hike within the Mount Baker Snoqualamie Forest. The trailhead can be accessed off of Mount Index Road near Stevens Pass. Make sure to get an early start in order to get a parking spot at the trailhead.

What the trail lacks in length, it makes up for in elevation gain. The first mile and a half winds across a well manicured and slightly inclined path, at the end of which you can...

searchResults.area_name: Mount Si Natural Resources Conservation Area
searchResults.name: Mount Si Trail
searchResults.location_label: Mount Si Natural Resources Conservation Area, Washington
searchResults.description:
The Mount Si Trail is one of the most popular trails in Washington, and for good reason. Less than an hour from Seattle, the summit offers incredible views of the Snoqualmie River Valley and the surrounding area.

There is a very large parking area at the trailhead, but it does fill up on warm weekends. Per usual, it’s best to arrive early. A Discover Pass is required.

The hike is hard enough to be a challenge, but not so difficult that weekend warriors can’t climb it. The trail is almost...

searchResults.area_name: Middle Fork Snoqualmie Natural Resources Conservation Area
searchResults.name: Mailbox Peak Trail Loop
searchResults.location_label: Middle Fork Snoqualmie Natural Resources Conservation Area, Washington
searchResults.description:
Mailbox Peak Loop is a challenging hike in the North Bend area that combines the Old Trail with the New Trail created by the Department of Natural Resources.

The Old Trail is very challenging with sharp inclines all the way up through the forest. The trail was easy to follow until about 3000 feet where it gets covered by falling pines and leaves. Luckily the White Diamond Trail markers are well placed and easy to follow. Once the Old Trail meets the New Trail it's pretty straightforward from...

searchResults.name: Burroughs Mountain Loop Trail
searchResults.description:
This trail has great views of Mount Rainier, especially from the second vista. It is mostly a sandy trail but there are rocky sections. You will pass two lakes along the way: Frozen Lake and Shadow Lake. Keep an eye out for wildlife such as mountain goats, pika, and black bears. There is not a lot of shade so be sure to bring sunscreen and water. You can also camp at Sunrise Camp on the south side of the loop.

This is one of Mount Rainier National Park's many backpacking routes. A wilderness...

searchResults.name: Granite Mountain Trail
searchResults.location_label: Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest, Washington
searchResults.description: Granite Mountain Trail in Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest follows a steep route up with some light scrambling to the beautiful summit of Granite Mountain, where hikers will find a lookout cabin/fire tower. Visitors will be treated to spectacular views of the surrounding alpine lakes and peaks, including Mount Rainier on a clear day to the south. This trail travels through potential avalanche terrain, so research conditions before heading out during winter months. A Federal Northwest...

searchResults.name: Gem Lake via Snow Lake and High Lakes Trail
searchResults.location_label: Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest, Washington
searchResults.description: This is a popular trailhead, so an early start is recommended on the weekends. You will climb up a series of switchbacks to reach the top of the pass and drop down towards Snow Lake. After you stop and rest by the water, continue up another climb that has some steep rocky sections to reach Gem Lake. There are some spots to pitch tents at both lakes. Use caution on the way back down, as there is some loose rock and the trail can get busy with several other hikers.

searchResults.name: Blanca Lake Trail
searchResults.location_label: Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest, Washington
searchResults.description:
Blanca Lake Trail is a popular mountain trail through the Wild Sky Wilderness and the Henry M. Jackson Wilderness. The hike to Blanca Lake offers many fantastic views. On a clear day, visitors will be treated to great views of Glacier Peak and Columbia Peak across the valley.

The highest point of the trail is at Virgin Lake, and then descends to the southern shore of the gorgeous blue lake below. Once you reach Blanca Lake, views of Monte Cristo can be seen. Waterfalls feed the lake from the...

searchResults.name: Lake Ingalls via Ingalls Way Trail
searchResults.description:
There is a small parking lot at the trailhead for the Ingalls Way Trail, and it can fill up quickly in the summer as this is one of the more popular hikes in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness. Some parking may be allowed along the road, but please do not park on both sides of the road as this blocks emergency vehicle access. 

The first half of the hike is pretty easy, the elevation gain is gradual, and the trail is in good condition.  Just under half a mile into the trail you will come to a...

searchResults.name: Gothic Basin Trail to Foggy Lake
searchResults.location_label: Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest, Washington
searchResults.description:
Weden Creek (Gothic Basin) Trail #724 starts in Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest and traverses into Morningstar National Resources Conservation Area managed by the Washington State Department of Natural Resources. The trail ends at Foggy Lake where experienced mountaineers gain access to climb Del Campo and Gothic Peaks. Camping is allowed in the basin and surrounding area, but campfires are prohibited.

Start at the Barlow Pass Trailhead and pass the metal gate just south of the parking....

searchResults.name: Mount Washington Trail
searchResults.location_label: Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest, Washington
searchResults.description: The best route off the interstate is to take exit 38 to reach the trailhead. The trail climbs through the forest and passes a steep rocky outcropping about a half mile from the trailhead. This spot is popular with rock climbers and you may notice climbing hardware hanging from the cliff. As you ascend you are rewarded with some views, the summit has a perfect view of Mt. Rainier.

searchResults.name: Mount Townsend Trail
searchResults.description: The road to the trailhead is mostly paved with some gravel spots. There is no cell service on the road to the trailhead. The road is a single track with plenty of spots to pull out and let cars pass. There are big potholes in the paved road but they’re marked pretty clearly with yellow paint. When you finally get to the end of the paved forest service road and take the left to the trailhead it turns to gravel again and this past half a mile or so had the worst potholes of the whole trip. It's...

searchResults.name: Rachel and Rampart Lakes Trail
searchResults.description:
The beginning of the trail can be tricky due to the switchbacks. It is very easy to miss turns and end up walking on an old abandoned forest road. Downloading offline maps can help you navigate this trail. The trail is a challenging journey with a lot of elevation gain, and the path is known for its steep, rocky sections. Be prepared for a challenge, and wear sturdy hiking boots to help with the rugged terrain.

Rachel Lake is serene and even more beautiful from the vantage up the trail...

searchResults.name: Mount Dickerman Trail
searchResults.location_label: Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest, Washington
searchResults.description: This loop begins in the forest and eventually breaks out of the trees the higher you get. Expect a continuous climb from the parking area until you reach the summit. There are stunning views along the ridgeline that head towards the top. From here you will have 360-degree views of the surrounding mountains.

searchResults.area_name: Middle Fork Snoqualmie Natural Resources Conservation Area
searchResults.name: Dirty Harry's Peak via Birdhouse Trail
searchResults.location_label: Middle Fork Snoqualmie Natural Resources Conservation Area, Washington
searchResults.description: Harry's Peak can be accessed via Birdhouse trail. The old trailhead to Harry's Peak is now completely overgrown and no longer open. Some overgrowth will also be experienced on the trek between Dirty Harry's Balcony to Dirty Harry's Peak. Please use Birdhouse Trail on ascent and descent to avoid unsafe conditions.

searchResults.name: Bandera Mountain Summit
searchResults.location_label: Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest, Washington
searchResults.description: Follow the Ira Spring Trail uphill for a little over 2.5 miles to a point where there's a sign pointing out Mount Bandera. From this point, you will follow along the ridge towards Little Bandera Mountain. After this, there is a social trail leading to the summit of Bandera Mountain. To summit the mountain, you will need to tackle a steep and rocky incline, so make sure you come prepared with proper footwear. Downloading the map ahead of time is helpful for navigating off-trail.

searchResults.name: Rattlesnake Ledges to East Peak
searchResults.description: This route takes hikers to the popular Rattlesnake Ledge, and then continues on to the summit of East Peak. There are awesome views of Rattlesnake Lake and the nearby mountains from the series of ledges. At East Peak, there is a bench in place to sit and enjoy the view from. The trail is well-maintained and easy to follow.

searchResults.area_name: Mount Si Natural Resources Conservation Area
searchResults.name: Mount Teneriffe via Kamikaze Trail
searchResults.location_label: Mount Si Natural Resources Conservation Area, Washington
searchResults.description: The ascent of Mount Teneriffe via the Kamikaze Trail is a thrilling and rigorous ridge-summit adventure in the Mount Si Natural Resources Conservation Area near North Bend, Washington. You’ll begin on forested logging-road grade toward Teneriffe Falls, then shift into the “Kamikaze” section—an unrelenting climb, often steep and exposed, straight up toward the ridge crest. Higher up, you’ll traverse a ridge with sparse vegetation, loose rock, and dramatic exposure before reaching the summit at...

searchResults.name: Crystal Lakes Trail to Sourdough Gap
searchResults.description:
This trail begins with a forested, shady, steep climb up some switchbacks. Your efforts are rewarded when you Lower Crystal Lake, which is a great spot to stop for a break and a snack. Continue on to Upper Crystal Lake and Sourdough Gap for more beautiful views.

This is one of Mount Rainier National Park's many backpacking routes. A wilderness permit is required for all overnight stays in the backcountry. A climbing permit is required for all travel above 10,000 feet or on glaciers....

searchResults.name: Three Summits Loop Trail
searchResults.description: This loop takes hikers over the top of West Tiger #3, #2, and #1. Some areas have been logged offering impressive views of Mount Rainier along the way. While steep at points, the trails are well-defined and maintained routinely. Long stretches are through dense forest, so it is a great place to practice plant identification.

searchResults.name: Trout Lake, Copper Lake, Lake Malachite
searchResults.description: This is a perfect hike for those who like to cool off in alpine lakes. The first lake you will pass is Trout Lake, after this you will begin hiking up a series of swithbacks and pass some waterfalls. At the top you can visit Copper and Malachite Lake, which are very close to each other. A permit required for overnight camping.

searchResults.name: Shriner Peak Lookout Trail
searchResults.description:
The Shriner Peak Trail not only provides incredible views of the park’s eastern wilderness but also challenges hikers with a good workout. The 360-degree panorama from the summit is one of the best in the park.


The wilderness campsites at Shriner Peak are located near the lookout and offer an incomparable view of Mount Rainier at sunrise. Well worth the effort of waking early! 

A spring located about one mile back down the trail is the only source of water for this camp. Treat water before...

searchResults.name: West Tiger #1 and # 2 via High Point Trail
searchResults.description:
The West Tiger #1 and #2 hike offers a chance to visit some of the most picturesque areas via the High Point Trail. Particularly after rain, the trail may be muddy and damp.

On the High Point Trail, hikers first cross a powerline and then enter a verdant evergreen forest. The trail then meanders through patches of shaded wildflower meadows dotted with tall grasses. Hikers can stop along the road to see colorful wildflowers and berries. Bears are occasionally sighted in the region.

searchResults.name: Duckabush River Trail to Five Mile Camp
searchResults.description:
This trail follows the Duckabush River into The Brothers Wilderness and enters Olympic National Park around 6.7 miles.

The trail enters a burned area at around the two-mile mark and continues beyond Five-Mile Camp, the origin of the 2011 fire. Use caution when traveling and camping within the burned area, as there are many dead-standing trees and unstable surfaces as a result of the fire.

The trail is an easy grade for 2.4 miles and becomes more difficult while climbing over Big Hump. The...

searchResults.name: Crystal Peak Trail
searchResults.description: This is a must-do hike for visitors to Mount Rainier National Park. The climb is challenging, but the views are worth your effort. Climb this peak for terrific views of Hood, St. Helens, Chimney Rock, Adams, Glacier Peak and of course the massive frame of Mt. Rainier up the valley. Keep an eye out for wildflowers along the trail in the early summer.

searchResults.name: Hex Mountain Trail
searchResults.description:
Starts on a wide gravel/dirt path. Turns off onto a smaller trail after a mile or so. Weaves along a ridge with the lake to your left. Tons of wildflowers along the path. Steady incline the whole way up. There’s very little tree cover in the first 2 miles so on a sunny day it will be hot and unforgiving. Shade starts about 2/3rds of the way up when you’re on the final ridge section toward hex and the brush gets thicker.

Lovely hike if you’re looking for an easy summit with a simple trail to...

searchResults.area_name: Mount Si Natural Resources Conservation Area
searchResults.name: Mount Si via Teneriffe Connector
searchResults.location_label: Mount Si Natural Resources Conservation Area, Washington
searchResults.description: The Teneriffe Connector Trail offers breathtaking vistas as it meanders through a lush, rolling forest. Ascending will take you to several noteworthy vantage locations. A section of the trail offers views of Rattlesnake Ledge and Mount Rainier. There's another overlook with panoramic views and a rest area indicated by an enormous stone bench. The trail eventually leads to a viewpoint from which you can get a good view of Mount Si. On clear days, the trail offers views of Whistler Mountain and...

searchResults.name: Eagle Peak Trail
searchResults.description:
The first two miles of the Eagle Peak trail is a gradual ascent through a covered forest. Hikers will then reach a stream that diverts them to a beautiful open meadow. Afterward, climb up the saddle with a steep and rocky ascent where you will catch incredible views. Use caution while at the summit as it is very exposed and could become dangerous in unpredictable conditions.

The Native American name for Eagle Peak is Sim-layshe, which means Eagle. At the peak, the north faces views of Mount...

searchResults.name: Mount Baldy via Domerie Divide Trail
searchResults.description: This trail is a steep climb, followed by a scenic walk along the side of a mountain ridge to the summit of Mount Baldy. Trekking poles are recommended to help navigate the steep and rocky terrain. Go on a clear day for the best views. There are beautiful views of Lake Kachess and Lake Easton, Mount Rainier, Glacier Peak, and the Enchantments.

searchResults.name: Surprise Creek Trail to Surprise and Glacier Lakes
searchResults.location_label: Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest, Washington
searchResults.description: You will walk a short distance up a forest road to access Surprise Creek Trail. You will hike up the valley until the trail joins the Pacific Crest Trail near Surprise Lake. Continue along Surprise Creek until it joins the PCT a second time and passes Glacier Lake. Glacier Lake is probably considered the prettier of the two lakes.

searchResults.name: Lila Lakes Trail
searchResults.description: Begin from the Rachel Lake Trailhead and begin following the Rachel Lake Trail. At points the trail parallels Box Canyon Creek. After about 2 miles the trail gets steeper as you approach Rachel Lake. After passing this lake you will pick up the Lila Lake Trail. The final stretch leading to the lake is rocky. There are plenty of good camping areas near the lakes.

searchResults.name: Putvin Trail to Lake of the Angels
searchResults.description: The trail to Lake of the Angels via the Putvin Trail begins with a forested riverside walk but quickly turns into a demanding ascent into alpine terrain. A steep headwall section requires scrambling on roots and rock, and the path gains substantial elevation over loose terrain. Past the crux, you’ll emerge into a high basin ringed by rugged peaks and snowfields, culminating at the lake itself, nestled beneath the cliffs of the Mount Skokomish ridge. Wildflowers bloom in summer and marmots...

searchResults.name: Glacier Basin to Burroughs Mountain to Wonderland Trail Loop
searchResults.description:
This backpacking loop follows the Glacier Basin Trail up to the Burroughs Mountain Trail, to Sunrise Camp, and down the Wonderland Trail. This trail can be hiked in either direction. Hiking the loop counterclockwise can provide some beautiful views of Mount Rainier along the way.

This is one of Mount Rainier National Park's many backpacking routes. A wilderness permit is required for all overnight stays in the backcountry. A climbing permit is required for all travel above 10,000 feet or on...

searchResults.name: Rampart Lakes and Alta Mountain
searchResults.description:
The road leading to the trailhead is rough, narrow, and washed out in sections. 4WD is recommended. Keep in mind that this is not an official trailhead. The official Alta Mountain trail can be found here: https://www.alltrails.com/explore/trail/us/washington/alta-mountain-south-ridge-climbers-trail

This route is very steep in places and definitely a challenge, so it's a good idea to bring trekking poles to help navigate the incline. The trail is not well-marked or maintained, so downloading...

searchResults.name: Chelan Butte Trail
searchResults.description:
This trail takes you from Chelan Butte Trailhead Parking to Chelan Butte Peak. It’s a challenging hike with a steep incline, so sturdy footwear is recommended, as some parts can be slippery. It's best to hike on a colder day or start early to avoid the heat, as there’s no shade.

Your effort will be rewarded with great views of Lake Chelan and the Columbia River. During the spring, you'll find beautiful wildflowers. Keep an eye out for bighorn sheep and other wildlife.

searchResults.name: Van Trump Trail
searchResults.description:
The Van Trump Trail takes you to Comet Falls and is well-maintained and fairly gradual. The last mile or so gets steep and rocky, which can be hard on your knees. Trekking poles are recommended. The falls are beautiful and 320 feet tall.

The road to the trailhead can be dangerous in the winter. Parking is on the road to Paradise and space in the lot is limited.

searchResults.name: Dirtyface Peak Trail
searchResults.description: The trailhead starts right behind the Lake Wenatchee Ranger Station. Less than a mile from the trailhead there is a waterfall along Fall Creek. After this, you will hike up a long series of switchbacks with views of Lake Wenatchee. From the top, there is a viewpoint with an awesome view of the surrounding mountains.

searchResults.name: Talapus Lake via Pratt Lake Trail
searchResults.location_label: Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest, Washington
searchResults.description:
This trail is a scenic rocky climb that passes along many streams with some great unexpected waterfalls.

Once you take the turn at the junction, it's all downhill towards the lake. There are a few nice spots to sit and have lunch.

To head back, there is a good solid climb back up to the main trail followed by a nice descent.

searchResults.name: Lake Lillian Trail
searchResults.description: Follow the trail uphill through the forest with intermittent views of the surrounding mountains. The first lake you will arrive at is Twin Lakes. After this, you will enter into the Alpine Lakes Wilderness and arrive at Lake Lillian. The lake is an awesome place to cool off during the warmer summer months.

searchResults.name: Carne Mountain Trail
searchResults.description:
The trail starts 0.25 miles up Phelps Creek Trail #1511 and climbs switchbacks through open forests for about 2.5 miles. The forest opens up to a small mountain basin at about 6100 feet, where there are many campsites and an easily accessible stream.

The trail continues beyond the basin, past the junction with Old Gib Trail #1528, and switchbacks up to the south ridge of Carne Mountain. This is also where the trail enters the Glacier Peak Wilderness. You can follow the gentle ridge north to...

searchResults.name: Badger Valley to Grand Lake and Grand Pass Loop
searchResults.description: Dogs are not allowed on this trail, but may be allowed on other trails within the park. For more information, please visit: https://www.nps.gov/olym/planyourvisit/pets.htm

searchResults.name: Deer Ridge Trail
searchResults.description:
This route takes you on a steady incline to Deer Park Campground past Deer Ridge. Its terrain consists of shade, open meadows, and well-maintained paths. 

The trail can be hiked from either direction. If done bottom up, as most do, no fees are required. If done top-down, a pass is needed for the car windshield.

searchResults.name: Adventure, Poo-Poo Point, and West Tiger Trails Loop
searchResults.description: This adventurous trail explores both Tiger Mountain State Forest and West Tiger Mountain Natural Resource Conservation Area. The journey begins from East Sunset Way Trailhead, where parking is available. You'll encounter beautiful views, streams, and small babbling waterfalls as you navigate the trail. The forest canopy above provides ample shade throughout the hike. In spring, vibrant wildflowers are abundant along the trail.

searchResults.name: Silver King via Crystal Lakes
searchResults.description: This is an alternate route up the south ridge to the summit of Silver King. It is an advanced route that requires some Class 2 scrambling on scree. Starting on the Crystal Peak Trail switchbacks steeply climb through the forest and continue to ascend to Lower and Upper Crystal Lakes. Both alpine lakes have backcountry campsites. Close to 3.5 miles, the scramble and off-trail section heads north to the summit of Silver King.

searchResults.name: Cathedral Rock Trail
searchResults.description: Begin by hiking up the switchbacks on the Cathedral Pass Trail leading to Nosh Nosh Wahtum. At the junction with the Pacific Crest Trail, begin to follow the PCT uphill, and Cathedral Rock will be visible looming above. A social trail leads to the base of Cathedral Rock, and to get to the summit, it requires a little scrambling, so come prepared. The summit has a fantastic view. Downloading the map ahead of time is helpful to find the spur trail leading to the Cathedral.

searchResults.name: Poo Poo Point, West Tiger, and TMT Loop
searchResults.description: Chirico Trail up to Poo Poo Point is usually heavily populated, especially on the weekend. Once you get past that and onto TMT you'll enjoy the solitude of the forest. From the top of West Tiger #3, there are views of Seattle and of Mont Rainier on a clear day. A wooden bench is in place to sit and rest.

searchResults.name: Lake Ann via Esmerelda Basin Trail
Switchback Trail to Klahhane Ridge and Lake Angeles
searchResults.description:
This is a gorgeous backcountry loop to Lake Angeles. It is highly advised to have experience in the backcountry before attempting this adventure. The singletrack trail is extremely steep, with two big climbs. From Hurricane Ridge Road, the Switchback Trail climbs to Klahanne Ridge. 

From here, you will be above treeline for hours—it is crucial to know the weather and not attempt this ridgeline in inclement weather. When the weather is right, this is an incredibly scenic stretch of trail....

searchResults.name: Johnson Ridge Trail
searchResults.description: This is a beautiful trail that follows along a ridgeline, summits both Sunrise and Scorpion Mountain and ends at Joan Lake. There are stunning views along the way. The trail is steep in the beginning, but the views are well-worth the effort. It is recommended to go on a clear day for the best views. The road to the trailhead can be challenging for low clearance vehicles.

searchResults.name: Snoqualmie Tunnel Via Annette Lake Trailhead
searchResults.location_label: Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest, Washington
searchResults.description: This trail starts with a short, moderate hike up the first part of the Annette Lake Trail through a wooded area, featuring waterfalls and a wide wood bridge over the river. Once you reach the John Wayne Trail, you take a left. From there it is an easy slight incline walk to the tunnel. It is possible to extend your trip by continuing on in the tunnel until you wish to turn back.

searchResults.name: Trap Lake Trail
searchResults.description: Spur trail from the Pacific Crest Trail (PCT) south of Stevens pass to Trap Lake, a nice destination for backpackers. Begin at the Tunnel Creek Trailhead and follow the Tunnel Creek Trail until you reach the PCT. Beautiful mountain views along the way. There are lovely wildflowers in the late spring and early summer.

searchResults.name: West Tiger #1
searchResults.description: This route accesses West Tiger #1 via Chirico, Poo Poo Point, One View, and Tiger Mountain Trail. Poo Top Trail is very lightly used but remains in fairly good condition. From the summit, there are views of Seattle and Mount Rainier on a clear day. The trailhead begins near the paragliding landing zone.

searchResults.name: West Tiger #1 via Preston and Tiger Mountain Trail Loop
searchResults.description: This route takes you from High Point Trailhead through a second-growth forest on a challenging and steady incline to the second-highest of the Tiger Mountains. At the summit, you will find the Hiker's Hut Lookout, which is a hut that is now repurposed as a shelter for hikers but had its origins in the post-World War II era. Initially designed to protect guns on navy ships, it was repurposed as an equipment shelter.

searchResults.name: Pete's Creek Trail to Colonel Bob Peak Trail
searchResults.description: The rocky outcropping marking the summit of Colonel Bob has great views of the surrounding mountains and Lake Quinault. The trail follows Pete's Creek into the wilderness area and travels through the dense mossy forest. There are several creek crossings along the way, water levels fluctuate seasonally but they can be knee deep at times.

searchResults.name: Hibox Mountain via Rachel Lake Trail
searchResults.description: The hike to Hibox Mountain via Rachel Lake begins with a pleasant stroll along Box Canyon Creek to the serene Rachel Lake basin. From there, the route transforms into a serious ascent: a boot path climbs the exposed slopes of Box Ridge, traversing loose talus and gaining thousands of feet over a short distance. The final step involves a short scramble onto solid rock that serves as the summit block. At the top, you’ll be rewarded with broad views of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, including...

searchResults.name: Tamanos Mountain via Owyhigh Lakes Trail
searchResults.description:
This is a breathtaking hike. You will begin by switchbacking through the forest. Eventually, the trail opens up to a beautiful meadow and lakes. Once you pass the lakes prepare yourself for a steep climb to the summit along a narrow path. Trekking poles are recommended. Your efforts are rewarded with beautiful views along the way. The views from the summit will not disappoint.

This is one of Mount Rainier National Park's many backpacking routes. A wilderness permit is required for all...

searchResults.name: Mildred Point via Wonderland Trail
searchResults.description:
Start out on the Wonderland Trail on the north side of Longmire. You will take this until you come to a fork in the trail. You want to go left onto the Wonderland Trail towards Rampart Ridge as opposed to the Wonderland Trail towards Paradise. 

Continue on this trail. You will cross over The Road to Paradise. Eventually you will come to a couple of switchbacks before make your way to a sign for the Comet Falls-Van Trump Trail. The sign will tell you that you have 3.2 miles to Mildred Point....

searchResults.name: Longs Pass Trail
searchResults.description: Longs Pass is accessed by following the Ingalls Way Trail uphill. You will cross over Longs Pass and into the drainage with Ingalls Creek running through it. The trail ends at the junction with the Ingalls Creek Trail near Ingalls Creek Camp. The views from Longs Pass offer a stunning view of Mount Stuart.

searchResults.name: Little Quilcene Trail to Mt. Townsend Trail
Esmeralda Basin Trail
searchResults.description: The parking area is along the North Fork Teanaway River. From this point, you will travel uphill through stands of pine trees, eventually climbing above treeline. After dropping over Fortune Creek Pass you will follow the trail to the junction with Fortune Creek Jeep Trail. After reaching this point, retrace the route back.

searchResults.name: Lillian Ridge Way Trail
searchResults.description:
Hike a very difficult and strenuous trail to Lillian Ridge in Mount Rainier National Park. The trail is challenging and has a lot of elevation gain, and it is known for its steep, rocky sections. Be prepared for a challenge, and wear sturdy hiking boots to help with the rugged terrain.

There is an 8-mile ride on a dirt road to get to the trailhead.

searchResults.name: Grand Lake via Badger Valley Trail
Hope, Mig, and Josephine Lakes via PCT
Basin Lake via Bullion Basin Trail
searchResults.location_label: Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest, Washington

searchResults.name: Lundin Peak View and Red Mountain via Old Commonwealth Trail
searchResults.location_label: Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest, Washington
searchResults.description: Technical rock scrambling and high-consequence exposure define the final ascent of this route, which should only be attempted by those with alpine climbing experience. Using a rope, harness, and helmet is strongly recommended for the vertical Class 4 and low Class 5 sections near the summit where fall risks are extreme. The journey begins on the Old Commonwealth Trail, a primitive and often overgrown path that provides a more rugged alternative to the Pacific Crest Trail. You will traverse...

searchResults.name: Goat Peak via Goat Creek Trail
searchResults.description: Starting out at Cougar Flat trail head up the Goat Creek Trail through the forest canopy towards American Ridge. The first 2 miles have a generally moderate incline, then it levels off before a steadier incline to American Ridge junction. Turn right on American Ridge Trail and follow the singletrack up to the top of Goat Peak. The views are wonderful and there is quite a bit of shade throughout the hike.

searchResults.name: Scatter Lake Trail
searchResults.description: This route climbs from the trailhead in the heart of the Lake Chelan-Sawtooth Wilderness into a remote mountain basin, culminating at the shimmering waters of Scatter Lake. You begin in lower forested slopes and quickly gain elevation as the trail traverses through an old burn area, then switchbacks steeply up into alpine meadow and larch-wooded terrain. As you ascend closer to the lake, you’ll cross steep slopes and sometimes loose rock underfoot, finally arriving in a high-ridge basin where...

searchResults.name: Merritt Lake Trail to Lost Lake
searchResults.description: This is a beautiful and challenging trail to two alpine lakes. Most people only go to Merritt Lake because Lost Lake adds more than a thousand feet of elevation gain in the out-and-back, but it is worth the extra trek. The lake sits below the gorgeous Mount Mastiff and offers solitude for camping. This route is challenging enough that it is recommended to have hiking experience before attempting.

searchResults.name: Rampart Ridge, Mildred Point, and Comet Falls
searchResults.description:
This is a steep and challenging route, but your efforts are rewarded with breathtaking views of Mount Rainier. There are many viewpoints along the way to take breaks and take in the scenery. Comet Falls is beautiful, especially in the early summer when there is the most water. Keep an eye out for wildflowers in the early summer.

There are a few logistics involved in hiking this route, especially if you don't want to walk to road back to Longmire. This point-to-point requires leaving two...

searchResults.name: Mount Ruth via Glacier Basin and Emmons Moraine Trails
searchResults.description: This loop begins by following the Glacier Basin Trail. Once you reach Glacier Basin Camp, head south for a steep climb to the top of the moraine. From there, follow the ridge up to the summit of Mount Ruth. Follow the moraine, via an unmaintained route until you join the Emmons Moraine Trail, which connects back to the Glacier Basin Trail. If you want to skip the off-trail section, you can make this hike an out and back and return the way you came. Enjoy beautiful mountain views and views...

searchResults.name: McNeil Peak via Russell Ridge Trail
searchResults.description: This challenging hike steeply climbs a singletrack trail through oak and pine forests. About 4 miles in, look for a cairn marking the turn for 6,641-foot McNeil Peak. There is no trail to the top, but the hiking is not technical, and if you have downloaded the map, it should be easy to find. While no advanced hiking skills are required for this route, the trail is relentlessly steep, and the summit is a bit exposed. It is recommended that only experienced hikers attempt this summit.

searchResults.name: Basin Lake and Crown Point Loop
searchResults.description:
This beautiful backcountry route starts on the Chutes and Ladders Trail and heads southwest through the forest. It continues south on the Bullion Basin Trail, climbing out of the woods and onto the Pacific Crest Trail to loop around the south flank of Crown Point. The Basin Lake Trail heads north, offering gorgeous views as it cuts through the high country and loops back to the PCT.

This route spends much of its time in the high country and is exposed to the elements. Consider it on a good...

searchResults.name: Tin Cup Joe Falls, Derrick Lake, and Horseshoe Lake
searchResults.description: This is a rugged and remote route to a scenic waterfall and two alpine lakes. It starts on the Middle Fork Trail along the Snoqualmie River, crossing a footbridge and passing a primitive backcountry camp spot before reaching the junction with the Tin Cup Joe Falls Trail. The trail to the falls and the lakes is steep and technical, including off-trail segments, boulder hopping, and bushwhacking. In the early spring, there can be a lot of blowdowns, and in late summer, it can be overgrown,...

searchResults.name: Hawkins Mountain and Boulder De Roux Loop
searchResults.description: This is a beautiful and rugged backcountry trail to the top of a peak, through the alpine, and among the dense temperate forest. The Hawkins Mountain Trail steeply climbs switchbacks to a ridge and continues along the ridge for over 2.5 miles. Here, the route goes off-trail to the summit of Hawkins Mountain. This section is for experienced hikers—it requires hiking through loose talus and high alpine shrub. The Hawkins Mountains Mines Trail leads through the high alpine to Fortune Creek and...

searchResults.name: Melakwa Lake via Denny Creek Trail [CLOSED]
searchResults.location_label: Mount Baker Snoqualmie National Forest, Washington
searchResults.description:
Hike along Denny Creek, underneath I-90 past a couple of waterfalls, ending at a high alpine lake.

Snow is often found well into June further down the trail. If you are planning to do the full length of the trail, please bring appropriate gear and pay attention to any blazes/markings on trees, as the trail may be covered by snow. Downloading the map ahead of time is recommended to help navigate.

searchResults.name: Green Mountain Trail [CLOSED]
searchResults.description: Hike to a historical fire lookout at the top of Green Mountain in the Glacier Peak Wilderness. The trail begins by gradually climbing through an old growth forest before breaking out into huge green slopes in the summer. Incredible views of jagged peaks in every direction from the top including Glacier Peak, Mt Baker, Dome Peak, and many others.
